metadata.title: Build a Study Audit Tool with Claude: Grade the Grade Using the Error Pattern

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I'm struck that the tool can flag all three error patterns at once, but it doesn't tell me which one to fix first. On my last exam Genetics was a coverage gap AND my Q3-Q4 miss rate spiked, so I have both a topic hole and a spacing failure. Help me: (1) extend exam_audit.py to rank the three diagnoses by how much score each likely cost me, (2) explain how to disentangle a coverage gap that only appears in late-exam questions, (3) output the single highest-leverage study adjustment.

props.command: I'm struck that the tool can flag all three error patterns at once, but it doesn't tell me which one to fix first. On my last exam Genetics was a coverage gap AND my Q3-Q4 miss rate spiked, so I have both a topic hole and a spacing failure. props.segment: Build a Study Audit Tool with

beats.narration_text: The graded exam is a precise diagnostic — it tells you exactly which topics you missed and what kind of errors you made. Most students look at the number and move on.

Three error patterns cover most exam failures: coverage gap — missed questions concentrated in specific topics, studied the wrong material. Cognitive operation mismatch — missed analysis and application but not recall, studied for the wrong level. Spacing failure — late-exam questions weaker than early-exam questions, material studied most recently, consolidated least. Each pattern has a specific study adjustment. The audit tool reads the diagnostic and names the pattern.
Ask Claude to write exam_audit.py with topic miss rates, question-type miss rates, quartile analysis, and a 3-row diagnosis table.

props.command: claude "Write exam_audit.py that takes a list of (question_num, topic, question_type, result) tuples — question_type is 'recall', 'application', or 'analysis'; result is 'correct' or 'incorrect'. Output: (1) miss rate by topic; (2) miss rate by question type; (3) miss rate by question number quartile (Q1-Q4 of exam); (4) a 3-row diagnosis table: Error pattern | What it indicates | Study adjustment. Include a 20-question demo."

beats.narration_text: The diagnosis logic: coverage gap if one topic has over 40 percent miss rate; cognitive operation mismatch if application and analysis miss rate is over 2 times recall miss rate; spacing failure if Q3 plus Q4 miss rate is over 1.5 times Q1 plus Q2.

props.code:
def diagnose(questions):
    # Miss rate by topic
    topic_miss = defaultdict(lambda: [0,0])
    for q_num, topic, q_type, result in questions:
        topic_miss[topic][1] += 1
        if result == 'incorrect': topic_miss[topic][0] += 1
    topic_rates = {t: m/n for t,(m,n) in topic_miss.items()}

    # Miss rate by question type
    type_miss = defaultdict(lambda: [0,0])
    for _, _, q_type, result in questions:
        type_miss[q_type][1] += 1
        if result == 'incorrect': type_miss[q_type][0] += 1
    type_rates = {t: m/n for t,(m,n) in type_miss.items()}

    # Quartile miss rate
    n = len(questions); q = n // 4
    quartiles = [questions[i*q:(i+1)*q] for i in range(4)]
    qmiss = [sum(r=='incorrect' for _,_,_,r in qt)/len(qt)
             for qt in quartiles]

    patterns = []
    # Coverage gap
    if max(topic_rates.values()) > 0.40:
        patterns.append(("COVERAGE GAP", max(topic_rates,
            key=topic_rates.get), "Extra sessions on flagged topic"))
    # Cognitive mismatch
    recall = type_rates.get('recall', 0)
    app_an = (type_rates.get('application',0) +
              type_rates.get('analysis',0)) / 2
    if recall > 0 and app_an > 2 * recall:
        patterns.append(("COGNITIVE MISMATCH",
            "Studied for recall, tested on application/analysis",
            "Practice problems and case studies"))
    # Spacing failure
    early = (qmiss[0]+qmiss[1])/2; late = (qmiss[2]+qmiss[3])/2
    if early > 0 and late > 1.5 * early:
        patterns.append(("SPACING FAILURE",
            "Late topics studied recently, consolidated least",
            "Spaced review of late-exam topics first"))
    return patterns

beats.narration_text: Demo run: 20 questions. Topic miss rates: Cell Biology 20 percent, Genetics 60 percent, Metabolism 25 percent. Diagnosis Row 1: COVERAGE GAP — Genetics 60 percent miss rate versus 22 percent overall. Question type miss rates: Recall 15 percent, Application 45 percent, Analysis 55 percent. Diagnosis Row 2: COGNITIVE OPERATION MISMATCH — studied for recall, tested on application and analysis. Quartile analysis: Q1 20 percent, Q2 25 percent, Q3 55 percent, Q4 60 percent. Diagnosis Row 3: SPACING FAILURE — late-exam topics were studied most recently and consolidated least.
Add --next-exam-plan flag that generates a targeted 5-day study schedule from the diagnosis.

--next-exam-plan output: Day 1 Genetics close-and-recall, no re-reading. Day 2 Genetics application problems, practice questions only. Day 3 Metabolism plus Cell Biology spaced second pass. Day 4 Genetics analysis problems, case studies. Day 5 PRACTICE TEST all topics timed. Plan directly derived from three diagnosed patterns: Genetics gets 2 extra sessions for coverage gap, practice questions dominate for cognitive operation mismatch, spaced distribution enforced for spacing failure. The diagnosis drove the plan — not intuition.

The post-mortem is the cheapest improvement available. The exam already contains the diagnosis; the audit tool reads it. Twenty minutes with a graded exam, mapped to its error pattern, reshapes the next month of studying in ways another hour of re-reading never would. The study adjustment is only as specific as the diagnostic data you enter.
Next: research the five-step planning process that prevents the cascade before the money moves.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Build a Study Audit Tool with Claude: Grade the Grade Using the Error ] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
